summary: Jeff Atwood traces the history of audio visualization software from its origins with Jeff Minter's 1984 Psychedelia through to the Xbox 360's Neon visualizer. He highlights how Minter's work on the Atari Jaguar CD-ROM's VLM-1 influenced an entire generation of PC visualization plugins, including WinAmp's popular Geiss and G-Force. The post also tells the story of G-Force creator Andy O'Meara, who was stuck on Navy submarine duty while his software became a phenomenon and got licensed by Apple for iTunes. Atwood concludes that despite the massive hardware improvements in modern PCs, visualization software like G-Force and Geiss hasn't evolved much beyond their 8-bit origins, while console-based Neon has leapfrogged them.
key_points: Jeff Minter pioneered audio visualization starting in 1984 and his techniques became the foundation for nearly all modern visualizers including WinAmp, Windows Media Player, and iTunes
The MP3 revolution and WinAmp made audio visualization mainstream on PCs in the late 1990s
G-Force creator Andy O'Meara had to serve on a nuclear submarine due to his Navy ROTC scholarship while his software became a phenomenon and landed an Apple licensing deal
Despite modern PC hardware capabilities, popular visualizers like G-Force and Geiss remain limited to 8-bit color with no hardware acceleration
Console-based visualization (Xbox 360's Neon) has far surpassed PC equivalents in visual quality
money_quotes: It took the MP3 revolution and WinAmp to make audio visualization mainstream.
One of the guys from Nullsoft came up to me and apologized for 'borrowing' the techniques I'd used on the Jaguar VLM for their own visualisations.

To this day much of the visualisation stuff that you see in the likes of Winamp, Windows Media Player and iTunes uses fundamentally the same technique – using feedback to amplify small source input dynamics – that I used in VLM-1.
For Andy O'Meara, creating a trend-setting computer graphics program has been a thoroughly depressing experience.
If I wasn't in the Navy, I'd be on tour with Seal working on visuals for him. Am I depressed about it? Yeah.
I suppose we'll have to wait another few years for the audio visualization developers to catch up to the hardware capabilities of newer PCs.
key_insight: One pioneering developer's work from the 1980s established the fundamental techniques still used in virtually all audio visualization software today, yet PC visualizers have barely evolved while console versions have leapfrogged ahead.
